coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of Zn0·1Cd0·9S heterostructure with N-doped graphene quantum dots and graphene for enhancing photoelectric performance in UV–visible light
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Jiang, Zicong
Lei, Yun
Lin, Yuanyuan
Hu, Jiaxin
Ouyang, Zhong
coredata.dc:description: In the present study, a heterostructure based on Zn0·1Cd0·9S, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), and graphene was successfully prepared by a simple method. Various analyses are conducted to determine the structure, morphology, and materials performance of the synthesized composite. The results exhibit that the Zn0·1Cd0·9S/N-GQDs/graphene heterostructure presents excellent photoelectric performance with a high photocurrent of 4.43 × 10−5 A/cm2 and 3.43 × 10−5 A/cm2 under light irradiation of 365 nm and 405 nm, respectively. It demonstrates a two-fold photocurrent enhancement in comparison to blank Zn0·1Cd0.9S. This remarkable improvement is ascribed to a mechanism in which the N-GQDs act as photosensitizers, enhancing the absorption ability. Concurrently, graphene serves as a carrier mobility substrate, facilitating the separation of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs. The synergetic effect between Zn0·1Cd0·9S, the N-GQDs, and graphene enhances the photoelectric performance. The Zn0·1Cd0·9S/N-GQDs/graphene heterostructure provides a new route for the enhancement of the photoelectric performance of a semiconductor under UV–visible light.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zn0.1Cd0·9S
N-GQDs
Graphene
Photoelectric properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of N-GQDs at different magnifications.
XRD and FTIR patterns of the N-GQDs.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum and XPS (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s spectra of the N-GQDs. (e) Schematic of the N-GQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the N-GQDs, and the inset contains optical photographs of the N–GQDs under the excitation with light of wavelength 405 nm, (b) PL spectra of the N-GQDs at various excitation wavelengths, (c) PLE spectrum of the N-GQDs at the detection wavelength of 570 nm, (d) the energy band diagram of the N-GQDs (top) along with associated absorption, PLE, and PL spectra at 470 nm (bottom).
(a) XRD patterns of ZCS, ZCS/NG, and ZCS/NG/GR. (b) TEM image of ZCS/NG/GR.
(a) SEM images, (b) EDS spectrum, and (c) elemental mapping images of the ZCS/NG/GR composite.
(a) Survey XPS spectrum and high-resolution (b) Zn 2p, (c) Cd 3d, (d) S 2p, (e) C 1s, and (f) O 1s spectra of ZCS/NG/GR.
FTIR spectra of ZCS, ZCS/NG, and ZCS/NG/GR.
Zeta potentials of ZCS, N-GQDs, GO, and ZCS/NG/GR.
UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of ZCS, ZCS/NG, and ZCS/NG/GR.

Time-dependent photocurrents of ZCS, ZCS/NG, and ZCS/NG/GR at the excitation wavelengths of (a) 365 nm and (b) 405 nm. Linear sweep voltammetry of the ZCS/NG/GR composite (c) in dark and light of 405 nm. The stability test of the ZCS/NG/GR composite (d) at the excitation wavelength of 405 nm.
Nyquist plots of ZCS, ZCS/NG, and ZCS/NG/GR.
Schematic of the charge transfer in the ZCS/NG/GR composite.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of Zn0·1Cd0·9S heterostructure with N-doped graphene quantum dots and graphene for enhancing photoelectric performance in UV–visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Resources and Environmental Engineering, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present study, a heterostructure based on Zn0·1Cd0·9S, N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs), and graphene was successfully prepared by a simple method. Various analyses are conducted to determine the structure, morphology, and materials performance of the synthesized composite. The results exhibit that the Zn0·1Cd0·9S/N-GQDs/graphene heterostructure presents excellent photoelectric performance with a high photocurrent of 4.43 × 10−5 A/cm2 and 3.43 × 10−5 A/cm2 under light irradiation of 365 nm and 405 nm, respectively. It demonstrates a two-fold photocurrent enhancement in comparison to blank Zn0·1Cd0.9S. This remarkable improvement is ascribed to a mechanism in which the N-GQDs act as photosensitizers, enhancing the absorption ability. Concurrently, graphene serves as a carrier mobility substrate, facilitating the separation of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs. The synergetic effect between Zn0·1Cd0·9S, the N-GQDs, and graphene enhances the photoelectric performance. The Zn0·1Cd0·9S/N-GQDs/graphene heterostructure provides a new route for the enhancement of the photoelectric performance of a semiconductor under UV–visible light.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past several decades, extensive studies have been dedicated to the research of various types of semiconductors, including metal chalcogenides, metal oxides, and complex metal oxides [1,2]. Particularly, the development of semiconductor composites that exhibit a high visible-light response and are not limited to ultraviolet light has received considerable attention, because visible light constitutes a major proportion of solar radiation [3,4]. Our previous works have revealed that ZnxCd1-xS ternary semiconductors possess tunable band gaps lying between those of ZnS and CdS [5,6]. Specifically, a ZnxCd1-xS semiconductor with a high content of Cd exhibits long-wavelength visible-light response and high photocorrosion resistance, which is proved to be a promising material for the visible light application [7,8]. Despite their good performances and stabilities, ZnxCd1-xS composites still suffer from rapid recombination of the charge carriers and low visible-light absorption, which hinder the effective utilization of visible light [9].

To overcome the carrier recombination issue, a novel heterostructure with comparatively more efficient interfacial charge transfer ability provides an effective strategy. Zhang et al. fabricated a Zn0·1Cd0·9S/Snln4S8 heterostructure with Eg of 2.4 eV, and used it for the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) and imidacloprid [10]. The photocatalytic efficiencies reached as high as 97% RhB and 55% IM within 60 min and 240 min, respectively, under visible light, because of the efficient separation of the carriers. Recently, metal-free carbon-based materials have attracted significant interest because they have the ability to efficiently improve the photoelectric properties owing to their excellent charge transport properties [11,12]. Graphene, a novel two-dimensional (2D) carbon (C) material, has received extensive attention owing to its extremely large surface area and high carrier mobility [13]. It has been reported that the graphene/semiconductor composites, such as graphene/WS2 [14], graphene/GaAs [15], and graphene/TiO2 [16], exhibit pronounced photoelectric performance in comparison to bulk materials.

An efficient strategy for improving the solar-energy utilization efficiency is to incorporate materials having high visible-light response. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) graphene fractals well-known for their quantum confinement and boundary effects [17]. Interestingly, unlike the zero band gap graphene, the band gap of GQDs is tunable, and can be regulated from 0 eV to 6 eV by varying the surface chemical defects [18]. Doping with heteroatoms can significantly tailor the electronic and chemical environments of their conjugated sp2 domains [19,20]. Among the various heteroatoms, nitrogen (N) atom possesses a comparable atomic size to C atom, which makes it easy to form N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) [21]. Most importantly, the rich valence electrons provide multiple configurations of chemical bonding, leading to abundant defect states, which are responsible for decreasing the band gap energy [22]. Jeon et al. demonstrated that the band gap of an N-GQDs has a functional relationship with the number of –NH2 groups [23]. N-GQDs have been explored as potential visible-light sensitizers. According to Tang's report, N-GQDs possess high absorption capacity for visible light, so that N-GQDs/g-C3N4 nanosheet composites have enhanced response intensities in the entire visible light region [24]. Zhang demonstrated that the absorption edge of a N-GQDs/BiOX heterostructure presented a red shift from ultraviolet region to visible light region in comparison to BiOX and also an increase in the absorption of visible light [4].

In view of these considerations, the photoelectric performance of Zn0·1Cd0·9S can be improved by forming a heterostructure with N-GQDs and graphene, in which the N-GQDs serve as sensitizers for improving the visible-light utilization efficiency. Moreover, graphene plays an important role in achieving more effective interfacial charge transfer, leading to a reduction in the electron–hole recombination efficiency. This strategy was investigated with the limited information provided. Herein, we prepared N-GQDs and graphene co-decorated Zn0·1Cd0·9S composites using the hydrothermal method and investigated the photoelectric properties in UV–visible light. The composites exhibited an enhancement in the absorption ability for UV–visible light and excellent photoelectric properties in comparison to the pure semiconductor. The roles of the N-GQDs and graphene in this system were investigated and a mechanism for the improvement of the photoelectric performance was proposed based on the experimental results.
All the chemicals were of analytical grade and used directly.

The raw graphite was chemically oxidized to graphite oxide by the modified Hummers’ method. The N-GQDs were prepared by the simple hydrothermal method demonstrated by Nan and his coworker with some changes [25]. Typically, 60 mg of graphite oxide were dispersed in 120 mL water, and the solution was sonicated for 30 min to obtain graphene oxide (GO). Subsequently, 2.4 mL ammonium hydroxide and 8 mL hydrogen peroxide were added to the GO solution. The final solution was placed in an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. The produced brown solution of N-GQDs was dialyzed for 48 h. The final N-GQDs solution was adjusted to 0.75 mg/mL in a rotary evaporator.

A facile hydrothermal process was performed for synthesizing the Zn0·1Cd0·9S/N-GQDs/graphene heterostructure at a low temperature in which cadmium acetate, zinc acetate, and thiourea served as Cd, Zn, and S sources, respectively. Briefly, 400 mg cadmium acetate, 768 mg zinc acetate, and 381 mg thiourea were dissolved in 80 mL water with ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. Subsequently, 1 mL (0.3 wt%) N-GQDs solution and 2 mg (0.9 wt%) GO were dispersed in the above solution with further ultrasonic treatment for 15 min. The mixture was placed in a 100 mL autoclave and maintained at 140 °C for 4 h. The final products were rinsed with ethanol several times and placed in a 60 °C oven for 2 h. The as-prepared composite is denoted hereafter as ZCS/NG/GR. Blank Zn0·1Cd0·9S (ZCS) and Zn0·1Cd0·9S/N-GQDs (ZCS/NG) composites were prepared by the same experiment process without adding the corresponding carbon source.

The morphology and structure were characterized via transmission emission microscopy (TEM) (JEM-2100F) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (D8 Advance), respectively. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) were performed on Gemini 300. UV–visible (UV–vis) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Lambda 750 S spectrophotometer and a Cary eclipse fluorescence spectrometer, respectively. Zeta potential tests were conducted by a Brookhaven BI-90 Plus Particle Size Analyzer. Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were measured on a Nicolet IS-10 FTIR spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed on Thermo ESCALAB 250XI. The photoelectric measurements were recorded using a standard three-electrode system on an electrochemical work station (CHI650e).

The N-GQDs were synthesized by the typical top-down strategy, with GO as the carbon source. The morphology of the N-GQDs is presented in Fig. 1 . The N-GQDs size distribution is in the range of 2–6 nm with a good spherical shape. The results exhibit that the average distribution of the N-GQDs is 3.5 nm. Moreover, lattice stripes of 0.21 nm can be observed, which can be indexed to the (100) plane of the N-GQDs.

The structure of the N-GQDs was confirmed via XRD and FT-IR spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, the N-GQDs exhibit a broad peak at 24°, which is ascribed to their (002) plane. The FT-IR peaks observed at 3444 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 are indexed to the characteristic absorptions of the O–H and C–OH groups [26]. The asymmetric absorption peak at 1600 cm−1 is indexed to the characteristic absorptions of CC and CO [27–29]. Particularly, the absorption peaks at 3160 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 can be indexed to the characteristic absorptions of N–H and C–N [30,31]. The results reveal that the heteroatoms of N are doped on the GQDs successfully and that the synthesized N-GQDs contain hydroxyl, amine, and carbonyl functional groups.

XPS characterization was conducted to further investigate the chemical state of the N-GQDs. The XPS survey of the N-GQDs is illustrated in Fig. 3 , which reveals an O 1s, a N 1s, and a graphitic C 1s peak, respectively. Fig. 3b–d demonstrate the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum is separated into three peaks centered at 284.4 eV, 285.7 eV, and 287.8 eV, which correspond to sp2 CC, sp3 C–O, and CO bond [32]. The N 1s spectrum is divided into three peaks, which indicate that the nitrogen atoms are present in three types of C–N bonding configurations. The binding energy peaks at 399.2 eV, 399.7 eV, and 401.3 eV were consistent with pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N, respectively [33]. A schematic of the N-GQDs is shown in Fig. 3e. Pyridinic N refers to an sp2 hybridized N with two C atoms (C–N–C), whereas pyrrolic N corresponds to an sp3 hybridized N a five-atom ring (C2–N–H). The formation of graphitic N (N–C3) is ascribed to the replacement of C by N in the graphene skeleton [34]. The O 1s region (Fig. 3d) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 530.8 eV and 532.2 eV, which can be assigned to C–OH and C–O–C bonds, respectively [35]. The obtained configuration contents of O, N, and C in N-GQDs from the XPS results are summarized in Table 1 . The doping concentration of the N-GQDs is 9.85 at%, indicating that N atoms are successfully incorporated in the GQDs. The high concentration of oxygen suggests a rich oxygen-containing functional group exists in the N-GQDs.

The optical data of the N-GQDs collected via UV–vis absorption spectroscopy are presented in Fig. 4 a. Typically, an absorption peak in the deep UV region at 239 nm (5.19 eV) is indexed to the Cπ–Cπ* transition of a CC bond. The peak at 303 nm (4.09 eV) is related to the Cπ–Oπ* electronic transition of a CO bond. N doping leads to a new energy level, which introduces additional peaks at 354 nm, 417 nm, and 481 nm (corresponding to the energies of 3.50 eV, 2.97 eV, and 2.58 eV, respectively). These additional peaks originating from the Cπ–Nπ* transition are related to C–N and CN bonds, which correspond to the three types of C–N bonding configurations identified from XPS. They lead to a substantially broadened absorption band in the visible light region [36,37]. To collect more optical data of the N-GQDs, their PL spectra were recorded under different excitation wavelengths (Fig. 4b). As the N-GQDs are irradiated from 390 nm to 470 nm, the fluorescence intensity increases to a maximum at 470 nm with a large Stoke's shift of 54 nm. Subsequently, it decreases as the excitation wavelength further increases to 590 nm, exhibiting clear excitation wavelength dependence. Concurrently, the PL peaks exhibit a redshift from 501 nm to 610 nm, ascribed to the strong electron affinity of the N atoms in the N-GQDs [38]. Under the irradiation of 405 nm, the N-GQDs present intense green luminescence (Fig. 4a inset). The PL excitation (PLE) spectrum of the N-GQDs (Fig. 4c) has a broad shoulder in the range of 300–400 nm and a strong peak at ~470 nm corresponding to the surface states, which is similar to the UV–vis results [20]. These results indicate that the surface states of the N-GQDs play a leading role in the PL behavior [39]. Based on the above optical behavior of the N-GQDs, possible absorption and emission transitions were proposed, as shown in Fig. 4d. The effective doping of N introduces new and different energy levels (N π*) that are close to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the GQDs. When the excitation energy is higher than 3.50 eV (354 nm), electrons are excited, leading to the electron transition of Cπ–Nπ*. Subsequently, the excited electrons return to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) following vibration relaxation between the same energy levels, resulting in the PL behavior, which is responsible for the red shift of PL under irradiation of decreased excitation energy [40].

To confirm the structure and crystalline quality of the composites, XRD and TEM characterizations were conducted, which are illustrated in Fig. 5 . The diffraction patterns of the composites present six peaks at 25.06°, 26.63°, 28.34°, 44.00°, 48.04°, and 52.18°, which can be indexed to a hexagonal structure corresponding to the (100), (002), (101), (110), (103), and (200) facets, respectively. The diffraction peaks of ZCS slightly shift toward larger 2θ angles in comparison to those of CdS, which indicates that Cd2+ ions are partly substituted by the small diameter Zn2+ ions, leading to a small interplanar spacing. Importantly, the strong and narrow peaks exhibit a good crystallinity of the sample. As can be observed from the XRD pattern, there are no notable peaks of graphene and the N-GQDs, which suggests that the incorporation with them has little effect on the crystal growth of ZCS. The TEM pattern (Fig. 5b) reveals the (100) phase of ZCS in 0.31 nm and (002) phase of graphene in 0.21 nm.

The morphology of ZCS/NG/GR was obtained via SEM. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, the ZCS particles are coated by graphene sheets, owing to the flexible characteristic. The heterostructure formation between graphene and the ZCS particles is beneficial for the interfacial electron migration because of the large area contact. To confirm the presence of the N-GQDs, the EDS spectrum was recorded, which is shown in Fig. 6b. The results confirm the existence of Cd, Zn, S, C, and N elements in the ZCS/NG/GR composite, with a N content of 3.29 at%. Their dispersion is presented in the element mapping displayed in Fig. 6c. C and N have similar elemental distributions to Zn, Cd, and S, revealing that the N-GQDs are successfully anchored on the ZCS particles.

XPS measurement of the ZCS/NG/GR composite was performed to confirm the chemical states of the elements, which are exhibited in Fig. 7 . The survey spectrum of the ZCS/NG/GR composite validates the presence of Zn, Cd, S, C, and O elements (Fig. 7a). The two peaks in the high-resolution Zn 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 7b) can be indexed to the spin–orbit coupling of Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2, which are centered at 1044.9 eV and 1021.8 eV, respectively [41]. The high-resolution Cd 3d XPS spectra are illustrated in Fig. 7c, where the two peaks at 404.3 eV and 411.0 eV are indexed to Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2, respectively. The XPS Zn 2p3/2 and Cd 3d5/2 peaks are assigned to Zn–S and Cd–S bonds, respectively, which indicate the presence of Zn2+ and Cd2+ in a sulfur surrounding environment [42]. The high-resolution S 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 7d) are resolved into two peaks at 160.6 eV and 161.8 eV, attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of S2− [43]. High-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 7e) are split into three peaks, ascribed to sp2 CC, sp3 C–O, and CO bonds located at 284.3 eV, 285.6 eV, and 287.9 eV, respectively. The O 1s region (Fig. 7f) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.1 eV and 532.8 eV, ascribed to C–OH and C–O–C bond, respectively. Compared to the N-GQDs, the bond energy of O 1s exhibits a slight shift, and the number of C–OH, CO, and C–O–C bonds have a remarkable decrease, indicating a part of oxygen-containing functional groups is removed in the hydrothermal process.

The functional groups of ZCS and the ZCS/NG and ZCS/NG/GR composites were identified via FT-IR spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 8 , all the FT-IR spectra exhibit an absorption peak at 3442 cm−1, which is ascribed to the characteristic absorption of an O–H bond. The peaks at 1629 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 are the vibration peaks of CO and C–OH bonds. CO and C–OH bonds are revealed in the ZCS composites owing to the residual acetate from the zinc acetate or cadmium acetate. Compared to the FT-IR spectrum of ZCS, those of ZCS/NG and ZCS/NG/GR exhibit an extra absorption peak centered at 1554 cm−1, corresponding to the vibration of a CC bond, originating from the N-GQDs and graphene [44,45]. No peaks of N–H and C–N bonds are detected in the composites, unlike in the N-GQDs, which is ascribed to the low content of the N-GQDs in the ZCS/NG and ZCS/NG/GR composites. In addition, no additional chemical bonds are formed after the coupling to the N-GQDs and graphene, indicating that the N-GQDs or graphene are anchored on the ZCS surface by a physical force.

To verify the results of FT-IR, the zeta potentials were measured, which are displayed in Fig. 9 . The N-GQDs have a high negative zeta potential. The can partially be explained by the doping with a high electronegativity N heteroatom. However, the primary cause is the number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface as well as the GO [46]. The high surface electronegativity is beneficial for the N-GQDs and GO to anchor on the ZCS surface by electrostatic attraction, leading to a decrease in the positive zeta potentials from 9.85 mV to 6.21 mV. The results of zeta potential reveal that the ZCS/NG/GR composite is synthesized by electrostatic self-assembly [47].

The optical absorption properties of the ZCS, ZCS/NG, and ZCS/NG/GR composites were determined via diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (Fig. 10 ). It can be clearly seen that the pure ZCS exhibits an efficient absorption edge at 557 nm with low visible-light absorbance. Compared to the pure ZCS, coupling with the N-GQDs has little effect on the intrinsic optical absorption edge of ZCS by the physical incorporation; however, it enhances the absorbance intensity in the entire UV–vis region. The results indicate that the presence of the N-GQDs enables additional light absorbance, which is beneficial to utilize more photons and increase the production of photogenerated electrons. The introduction of graphene has little effect on the light absorbance, indicating that it plays the role of a substrate for the nucleation of ZCS.

The photoelectric performances of the different composites were tested on an electrochemical work station equipped with light sources of 365 nm and 405 nm. Under the irradiation of 365 nm (Fig. 11 a), it can be clearly seen that the photocurrent of ZCS/NG is significantly improved, being 64% higher than that of pure ZCS. The significant enhancement can be attributed to the excellent electronic conductivity and quantum confinement effect of the N-GQDs. Moreover, following the introduction of graphene into the ZCS/NG composite, the photocurrent reaches a maximum of 4.43 × 10−5 A/cm2, which is over twice as high as that of pure ZCS. Under the light of 405 nm (Fig. 11b), the ZCS/NG/GR heterostructure achieves a high photocurrent of 3.43 × 10−5 A/cm2, which is an approximately two-fold photocurrent enhancement in comparison to that of blank ZCS. The superior photocurrent among all the systems indicates that the ZCS/NG/GR heterostructure has remarkable photosensitization efficiency in the visible-light region. Fig. 11c displays the anodic current as a function of the applied bias voltage under dark conditions and a light of 405 nm. It can be clearly seen that the output current (I = Ilight - Idark) of the ZCS/NG/GR heterostructure increases with the increase in the bias voltage, indicating a superior electron–hole separation among those of the considered structures. In addition, due to the strong light response of the N-GQDs and superior mobility of graphene [48], the ZCS/NG/GR heterostructure delivers a favorable photostability with less than 7% photocurrent decay under periodic on/off irradiation of visible light for 700 s (Fig. 11d).

To verify the mechanism of the photocurrent improvement, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was conducted to estimate the interfacial electron transfer properties of the composites. As displayed in Fig. 12 , all the samples show a well-defined semicircle and a straight line. The semicircle diameter in the high-frequency region is indexed to the interfacial charge transfer resistance (Rct) corresponding to an electron-transfer-limited process [49]. The linear portion at low frequency is fitted by the Warburg impedance (WR), which is a characteristic of a mass-diffusion-limited process [50]. To better understand the electrochemical process, an equivalent circuit was simulated (the inset), where CPE represents the double-layer capacitance and Rs denotes the solution resistance [31]. The Rct values of pure ZCS and ZCS/NG are 48.1 Ω and 42.6 Ω, respectively. The decrease in Rct is ascribed to the incorporation of the N-GQDs, which improve the surface wettability of ZCS by the presence of various functional groups (amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl), leading to a low interfacial charge transfer resistance [51]. With the further incorporation of graphene, the Rct of ZCS/NG/GR exhibits a minimum of 40.5 Ω, exhibiting the excellent improvement of the electrical conductivity. The low interfacial charge transfer resistance can increase the photocarrier separation, resulting in a high photocurrent.

To better understand the improvement of the photoelectric performance, a mechanism was proposed, which is illustrated in Fig. 13 . The enhanced photoelectric performance of ZCS/NG/GR can be described as a synergetic effect between the ZCS, N-GQDs, and graphene, resulting in enhanced light absorption and accelerated photoexcited electron transportation. In detail, under a series of light irradiation, ZCS can be radiated by UV and visible light, generating electrons and holes. Subsequently, the photoelectrons migrate from the conducting band of ZCS to graphene, because of its excellent carrier mobility characteristic, achieving the separation of the electron–hole pairs. Concurrently, the N-GQDs serve as photosensitizers [52] and absorb light with wavelength less than 590 nm, which is confirmed by the PL analysis. The HOMO electrons are excited to the LUMO, leading to an excited state of N-GQDs. Subsequently, the band configuration contributes to the electron transfer from the LUMO level of the N-GQDs to the conduction band of ZCS. The electrons are finally transferred to graphene on account of its natural characteristic. The presence of the N-GQDs increases the visible absorption to 590 nm, leading to a notable enhancement of the photoelectric performance in visible light.

In summary, the heterostructure composite of ZCS/NG/GR was synthesized by a facile process. The ZCS/NG/GR composite demonstrated excellent photoelectric performance with a high photocurrent of 4.43 × 10−5 A/cm2 and 3.43 × 10−5 A/cm2 under the light irradiation of 365 nm and 405 nm, respectively, which were two-fold photocurrent enhancements in comparison to blank ZCS. These enhanced performances could be indexed to the synergetic effect of the ZCS, N-GQDs, and graphene. The N-GQDs served as sensitizers for improving the visible-light utilization efficiency, which resulted in numerous photoinduced electrons. Concurrently, graphene played an important role in achieving more effective interfacial charge transfer, reducing the recombination efficiency of ZCS. Thus, this study provides an efficient strategy for the design of composites with enhanced photoelectric performances under UV–visible light.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge financial support of National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51204129).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Layered MoS2@graphene functionalized with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as an enhanced electrochemical hydrogen evolution catalyst
Chin. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Guo, L. Wang, S. Sun, Y. Wang, X. Chen, K. Zhang, D. Zhang, Z. Xue, X. Zhou, Layered MoS2@graphene functionalized with nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots as an enhanced electrochemical hydrogen evolution catalyst, Chinese Chemical Letters, 30 (2019) 1253-1260.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots as a green photosensitizer with Ag-doped ZnO nanorods for improved electrochemical solar energy conversion
Mater. Res. Bull.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Majumder, S. Dhar, K. Debnath, S.P. Mondal, Role of S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots as a green photosensitizer with Ag-doped ZnO nanorods for improved electrochemical solar energy conversion, Materials Research Bulletin, 93 (2017) 214-222.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Visible-light driven TiO2 photocatalyst coated with graphene quantum dots of tunable nitrogen doping
Molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Sun, H.-J. Li, N. Ou, B. Lyu, B. Gui, S. Tian, D. Qian, X. Wang, J. Yang, Visible-Light Driven TiO2 Photocatalyst Coated with Graphene Quantum Dots of Tunable Nitrogen Doping, Molecules, 24 (2019).
